skill.name: libreoffice-calc
skill.description: Use when creating, editing, formatting, or extracting LibreOffice Calc (.ods) spreadsheets via UNO, including cell operations, formulas, formatting, charts, named ranges, and data validation.
skill.summary: Automate LibreOffice Calc spreadsheet operations including cell manipulation, formatting, charts, and data validation via Python UNO bridge.
skill.author: dfk1352

security_audit.summary: All 88 static findings are false positives. The skill provides legitimate LibreOffice Calc automation via UNO API. Subprocess calls only launch LibreOffice with hardcoded arguments for spreadsheet operations. No cryptographic functions, no malicious code, no user input injection vectors.

high_findings.title: Misidentified Weak Cryptographic Algorithms
high_findings.description: 47 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings are false positives. The static scanner misidentified LibreOffice UNO API patterns (uno.createUnoStruct, storeToURL, etc.) as cryptographic functions. No cryptographic operations exist in this code.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Code inspection confirms all flagged lines use LibreOffice UNO API (createUnoStruct, storeToURL, loadComponentFromURL) - not cryptographic functions. No md5, sha, crypt, or hash functions present.

high_findings.title: Misidentified External Command Execution
high_findings.description: Subprocess calls are legitimate LibreOffice automation. The subprocess.run finds the LibreOffice binary and subprocess.Popen launches it headlessly - both with hardcoded arguments only.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: subprocess.run uses ['which', exe] to find LibreOffice - no user input. subprocess.Popen launches LibreOffice with hardcoded flags like --headless, --invisible - no injection risk.

high_findings.title: Misidentified Shell Execution in Documentation
high_findings.description: Backtick characters in SKILL.md are markdown code fences for documentation, not shell execution.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: All backticks in SKILL.md are part of markdown code blocks and inline code formatting. No shell commands are executed.

high_findings.title: Misidentified Temp Directory Access
high_findings.description: The /tmp directory access is for the legitimate snapshot feature that exports chart areas as PNG images.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: snapshot.snapshot_area() intentionally writes to /tmp for temporary image capture - this is a documented feature, not malicious file access.

high_findings.title: Misidentified System Reconnaissance
high_findings.description: The 'InvalidCellReferenceError' is a standard Python custom exception, not system reconnaissance.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 15 defines a custom exception class 'InvalidCellReferenceError' - standard Python exception handling, not reconnaissance.

content.value_statement: Automate LibreOffice Calc spreadsheet operations including cell manipulation, formatting, charts, and data validation. This skill provides a Python UNO bridge for programmatic spreadsheet creation and manipulation.
actual_capabilities: Create new LibreOffice Calc spreadsheets with Python
Read and write cell values with type support (text, number, date, formula)
Apply formatting including number formats, fonts, and alignment
Create and configure charts (line, bar, pie, and more)
Define and manage named ranges for easier formula references
Add data validation rules to cells (dropdown lists, numeric ranges)
limitations: Requires LibreOffice installation on the system
Cannot process encrypted or password-protected spreadsheets
Limited to operations supported by LibreOffice UNO API
Snapshot feature requires headless LibreOffice with PNG export support

use_cases.title: Automated Report Generation
use_cases.description: Generate formatted spreadsheets with data, calculations, and charts for recurring reports like sales summaries or financial statements.
use_cases.target_user: Business analysts and data professionals who create regular spreadsheet reports

use_cases.title: Data Migration and Conversion
use_cases.description: Convert data from other formats into LibreOffice Calc or export spreadsheets to PDF, Excel, or CSV formats.
use_cases.target_user: IT administrators and developers handling data migration tasks

use_cases.title: Spreadsheet Template Automation
use_cases.description: Create automated workflows that populate spreadsheet templates with data from external sources.
use_cases.target_user: Operations teams needing to fill standardized spreadsheet templates

prompt_templates.title: Create a New Spreadsheet
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a new LibreOffice Calc spreadsheet at {output_path} with a sheet named 'Data'. Add a header row with columns 'Product', 'Quantity', 'Price', and 'Total'.
prompt_templates.scenario: Initializing a new spreadsheet with structure

prompt_templates.title: Add Formatted Data
prompt_templates.prompt: In the spreadsheet at {path}, write the following data to sheet 1 starting at A2: {data_list}. Apply currency formatting to the Price column.
prompt_templates.scenario: Populating cells with data and formatting

prompt_templates.title: Create a Chart
prompt_templates.prompt: Add a line chart to the spreadsheet at {path} on sheet 'Sales'. The data range is A1:D10. Anchor it at cell F2 with size 8000x5000 (1/100mm units).

prompt_templates.title: Add Data Validation
prompt_templates.prompt: Add a dropdown validation to cells B2:B50 in sheet 1 of {path}. The dropdown should show options: 'Pending', 'In Progress', 'Completed'.
prompt_templates.scenario: Enforcing data consistency with validation rules

output_examples.input: Create a spreadsheet with sales data and a bar chart
output_examples.output: A new file 'sales_report.ods' is created with Sheet1 containing sales data in columns (Product, Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4). A bar chart is embedded anchored at row 10 showing quarterly trends.

output_examples.input: Apply currency formatting and add data validation
output_examples.output: Cells in the Amount column now display as currency ($1,234.56). Cells in the Status column show a dropdown with valid options.
The spreadsheet is converted to 'report.pdf' with all formatting, charts, and data preserved.

best_practices: Use absolute file paths for all spreadsheet operations (UNO requires absolute URLs)
Set PYTHONPATH to include the skill's scripts directory before importing calc modules
Use explicit type parameters when setting cell values ('number', 'text', 'date', 'formula')
Clean up snapshot PNG files after verification to prevent temp file accumulation
anti_patterns: Using relative paths - UNO API requires absolute file URLs
Forgetting to create the spreadsheet before writing cells
Mixing A1 and R1C1 coordinate systems without conversion
Passing chart positions as raw tuples without converting to UNO rectangle objects

faq.question: Does this skill require LibreOffice to be installed?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill requires LibreOffice to be installed on the system. It uses the UNO API to communicate with LibreOffice for all spreadsheet operations.

faq.question: Can I use this skill to edit existing Excel files?
faq.answer: Yes. You can load existing spreadsheets (both .ods and .xlsx formats) and modify them. Use the core module to load and edit existing files.

faq.question: What chart types are supported?
faq.answer: LibreOffice Calc supports multiple chart types including line, bar, column, pie, area, scatter, and more. Pass the chart type string to the create_chart function.

faq.question: How do I handle coordinate systems?
faq.answer: The skill uses R1C1 (row, column) addressing which is zero-based. A1 notation is not used directly. All positions are specified as (row, col) tuples.

faq.question: Can I export to PDF or other formats?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the export_spreadsheet function with format 'pdf', 'xlsx', or 'csv' to export to different formats.

faq.question: How do I capture visual snapshots of the spreadsheet?
faq.answer: Use the snapshot.snapshot_area() function to capture a cell-anchored area as a PNG image. This is useful for verifying chart placement and formatting.
